questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]\coordinate (P) at (0,2.4);\coordinate (Q) at (2.6,2.4);\coordinate (R) at (2.6,0);\draw (P)--(Q) node[midway,above] {$8$};\draw (Q)--(R) node[midway,right] {$9$};\draw (R)--(P) node[midway,below left] {$12$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled?

options.why: Assumes the equality holds without computing that $145\neq144$.

options.text: Right-angled, because $12$ is clearly the longest side.
options.why: Assumes any triangle with a longest side must be right-angled.

questions.solution_text: $8^2+9^2=145$ and $12^2=144$. Since $145\neq144$, the triangle is **not** right-angled.

options.why: Adds the numbers instead of comparing the squares.
Squares the largest number $13$ as a shorter side instead of as $c$.
Assumes the sum matches without computing that $205\neq225$.

options.text: A triad, since all three are whole numbers.
options.why: Assumes any three whole numbers form a triad without testing the squares.

questions.question_text: $(3,4,5)$ is a Pythagorean triad. Which of these is also a Pythagorean triad?

options.why: Adds $1$ to each number, which does not scale a triad by a common factor.
Adds $3$ to each number instead of multiplying every number by the same factor.

questions.solution_text: $(9,12,15)$ is $(3,4,5)$ with every number multiplied by $3$, so it **is** a Pythagorean triad.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (3.5,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3.3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$21$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$20$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$29$};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Is this triangle right-angled? The diagram is not to scale.

options.why: Makes an arithmetic slip; $400+441=841$, which equals $29^2$.
Adds the sides instead of comparing their squares.
